title: Hypatia - Myths and History - History for Atheists

Hypatia – Myths and History

# Hypatia – Myths and History

##### [April 1, 2024](https://historyforatheists.com/2024/04/hypatia-myths-and-history/ "3:12 am") [Tim O'Neill](https://historyforatheists.com/author/t-c-oneill2gmail-com/ "View all posts by Tim O'Neill") Comments [5 comments](https://historyforatheists.com/2024/04/hypatia-myths-and-history/)

T _he story often told about Hypatia of Alexandria was that she was a great scientist, rationalist and scholar who was brutally murdered by a mob of Christians who hated her knowledge and learning, with her death ushering in the Dark Ages. But this story is mostly nonsense and the real history is far more complex and much more interesting._ _Contrary to the myths, she was not a modern-style scientist, she was far from an atheist or what we would regard as a rationalist and her murder was due to the complex city politics of her day, not some hatred of science and scholarship._

For those who prefer the audio version, the Podcast edition is [now up on Buzzsprout](https://www.buzzsprout.com/1737374/14837538-hypatia-myths-and-history) or available on most podcast platforms. Or you can listen to it on the [History for Atheists podcast page](https://historyforatheists.com/podcast/) .

**Further Reading**

Alan Cameron, “Hypatia: Life, Death, and Works” in [_Wandering Poets and Other Essays on Late Greek Literature and Philosophy_](https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B015R4LFGQ/ref=as_li_tl?ie=UTF8&camp=1789&creative=9325&creativeASIN=B015R4LFGQ&linkCode=as2&tag=cladesvariana-20&linkId=ea8284e97b77343c75a512151df1310c) , pp. 37-80, (Oxford, 2016)

Thony Christie, [“Hypatia – What do we Really Know?”,](https://thonyc.wordpress.com/2019/01/09/hypatia-what-do-we-really-know/) Renaissance Mathematicus, 2019

Maria Dzielska, [_Hypatia of Alexandria_](https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/0674437764/ref=as_li_tl?ie=UTF8&camp=1789&creative=9325&creativeASIN=0674437764&linkCode=as2&tag=cladesvariana-20&linkId=4df24edbb32cae526a3f83e912470157) , (Harvard, 1995)

Peter Gainsford, [“Cosmos #3 – Hypatia and the Library”](http://kiwihellenist.blogspot.com/2018/12/cosmos-3-hypatia-and-library.html) , Kiwi Hellenist, 2018

Christopher Haas, [_Alexandria in Late Antiquity: Topography and Social Conflict_](https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/0801885418/ref=as_li_tl?ie=UTF8&camp=1789&creative=9325&creativeASIN=0801885418&linkCode=as2&tag=cladesvariana-20&linkId=083ccf66ccc28b924e0de623f487c4cc) (John Hopkins, 1997)

Spencer Alexander McDaniel, [“Who was Hypatia Really?”](http://talesoftimesforgotten.com/2018/08/06/who-was-hypatia-of-alexandria-really/) , Tales of Times Forgotten, 2018

Edward J. Watts, _[Hypatia: The Life and Legend of an Ancient Philosopher](https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/0190210036/ref=as_li_tl?ie=UTF8&camp=1789&creative=9325&creativeASIN=0190210036&linkCode=as2&tag=cladesvariana-20&linkId=2b98a3d9091d94e3b64fd7aa4148674a)_ , (Oxford, 2017)

Bryan J. Whitfield, [“The Beauty of Reasoning: A Reexamination of Hypatia of Alexandria”](http://math.coe.uga.edu/tme/issues/v06n1/4whitfield.pdf) , _The Mathematics Educator_ , Vol.6:1 (1995), pp. 14-21

4. **Hobby historian** says:
   
   [June 8, 2024 at 8:20 pm](https://historyforatheists.com/2024/04/hypatia-myths-and-history/)
   
   Good mythbusting. Good pedagogics. I could only come up with one very minor thing than it would be closer to the truth as we know it.
   
   Concerning Hypathia being depicted as a young and beautiful woman at the mercy of a mob  
   “Which tells us more about those 19th century painters and writers than it does about history.”
   
   I would say that it says more about the audience of those writers and painters. Few 19th century painters or writers could consider their own preferences first.

_“Getting history right is crucial, and no one – neither the religious nor the irreligious – should get a free ride when it comes to instrumentalising the past. Tim O’Neill’s forthright blog does a valuable job in keeping us all honest, and reminding us that historical evidence rarely behaves as one might want it to.” –_ Professor Tim Whitmarsh, A. G. Leventis Professor of Greek Culture at the University of Cambridge

_“A brilliantly erudite blog that stands sentinel against the wish-fulfilment and tendentiousness to which atheists, on occasion, can be no less prey than believers”_ – Tom Holland, best-selling history writer

_“Tim O’Neill’s blog is a fantastic place to turn for critical investigation of commonly-held assumptions about religion in the ancient world.”_ – Professor James F. McGrath, Butler University
